ENERGY EQUITY INTELLIGENCE FRAMEWORK¶

Integrated Analysis of Renewable Energy, Oil Markets & Macroeconomic Signals (2012–2025)¶

Prepared By: Deng Aguer Bul
Data Science Student | University of Tampa

Project Category: Financial Data Science • Energy Market Intelligence • Investment Analytics

Study Period: April 2012 – December 2025

Data Sources

  • Yahoo Finance
  • Federal Reserve Economic Data (FRED)

Technology Stack

  • Python
  • Pandas
  • NumPy
  • Matplotlib
  • Seaborn
  • Yahoo Finance API
  • Pandas DataReader
  • Jupyter Notebook

Core Focus Areas

  • Renewable Energy Equity Analysis
  • Oil & Gas Equity Analysis
  • Macroeconomic Analysis
  • Risk and Volatility Assessment
  • Market Signal Development
  • Investment Decision Support Systems
In [ ]:

Executive Summary¶

The energy sector is undergoing one of the most significant transformations in modern financial markets. While traditional oil and gas companies continue to play a critical role in global energy supply, renewable energy firms are becoming increasingly important as governments, businesses, and investors accelerate the transition toward cleaner energy sources. As a result, investors face a complex challenge: understanding how different segments of the energy market respond to changing economic conditions, commodity prices, and financial market trends.

This project develops the Energy Equity Intelligence Framework, a data-driven market intelligence system designed to analyze and compare the performance of renewable energy and oil & gas equities between 2012 and 2025. The framework integrates financial market data, commodity market indicators, and macroeconomic variables to explore whether certain economic and market conditions can help identify periods when energy stocks may become more attractive for investment research and portfolio consideration.

The analysis focuses on six representative energy companies. The renewable energy group includes NextEra Energy, First Solar, and Enphase Energy, while the traditional energy group includes ExxonMobil, Chevron, and ConocoPhillips. To provide broader market context, the study also incorporates Crude Oil Futures, the S&P 500 Index, and key macroeconomic indicators including the Federal Funds Rate, Consumer Price Index (CPI), Monthly 10-Year Treasury Yield, and Gross Domestic Product (GDP).

Using a structured financial analytics workflow, the project evaluates stock performance, monthly returns, volatility, drawdowns, macroeconomic sensitivity, and market relationships. Beyond descriptive analysis, it introduces a transparent signal-monitoring framework that examines how interest-rate conditions, oil market momentum, market pullbacks, and broader economic trends may be associated with future stock performance. These signals are then evaluated using forward-looking return analysis to assess their historical effectiveness as potential indicators of favorable market conditions.

Rather than attempting to predict stock prices with certainty, this project is designed as a decision-support and market intelligence tool. The framework demonstrates how financial data science, economic analysis, and quantitative research can be combined to transform large volumes of market data into actionable insights. The resulting analysis provides a foundation for investors, analysts, researchers, and energy market participants seeking to better understand the evolving relationship between energy equities and the broader economic environment.

Ultimately, this project illustrates how data-driven market intelligence can support more informed investment decisions, improve risk awareness, and provide a scalable framework for future extensions involving predictive modeling, portfolio construction, automated reporting, and real-time energy market monitoring.

In [ ]:

Disclaimer: This project is for research and portfolio demonstration purposes only. It is not financial advice or a recommendation to buy or sell securities.

In [ ]:

  18. Final Conclusion

1. Business Context and Motivation¶

The global energy sector is undergoing a historic transformation driven by technological innovation, evolving energy policies, changing consumer demand, and increasing investment in clean energy solutions. At the same time, traditional oil and gas companies continue to play a critical role in meeting global energy needs, creating a complex and dynamic investment landscape. As governments, businesses, and investors navigate the transition toward a more diversified energy future, understanding the factors that influence energy equity performance has become increasingly important.

Energy companies operate within a highly interconnected environment where stock performance is influenced not only by company-specific fundamentals but also by broader economic conditions. Interest rates, inflation, economic growth, commodity prices, and financial market sentiment all play significant roles in shaping investor expectations and capital allocation decisions. Renewable energy companies, for example, are often sensitive to financing conditions and long-term growth expectations, while oil and gas companies are more directly influenced by crude oil prices, global energy demand, and geopolitical developments. These differences create unique opportunities and risks for investors seeking exposure to the energy sector.

In today's financial markets, investors and analysts are challenged with identifying when energy equities may present attractive opportunities while managing the risks associated with market volatility and changing economic conditions. Professional investment firms, energy consultancies, hedge funds, and market intelligence organizations continuously monitor economic indicators, commodity markets, and equity performance to gain insights into potential investment opportunities and emerging market trends. The ability to transform large volumes of financial and economic data into actionable intelligence has become a critical competitive advantage.

This project was developed to address that challenge through a data-driven and systematic approach. Rather than focusing solely on historical stock performance, the project seeks to understand the broader economic and market forces that influence energy equities. By integrating stock market data, crude oil prices, and key macroeconomic indicators such as interest rates, inflation, GDP growth, and Treasury yields, the analysis provides a comprehensive view of how different segments of the energy sector respond to changing market conditions.

The Energy Equity Intelligence Framework serves as a market intelligence and decision-support system designed to evaluate renewable energy and oil & gas equities through the lenses of performance, risk, macroeconomic sensitivity, and signal-based analysis. The framework compares leading companies across both sectors and investigates whether specific economic and market conditions have historically been associated with stronger future stock performance. By doing so, the project moves beyond traditional descriptive analysis and toward a more practical application of financial data science.

Ultimately, this project aims to demonstrate how data analytics, economic research, and market intelligence can be combined to support more informed investment decision-making. The insights generated from this analysis can help investors, researchers, and industry professionals better understand the evolving relationship between energy markets and the broader economy while providing a foundation for future predictive models, investment strategies, and automated market monitoring systems.

In [ ]:

2. Research Objectives and Key Research Questions¶

The primary goal of this project is to develop a data-driven market intelligence framework for evaluating renewable energy and oil & gas equities under different economic and market conditions. By integrating financial market data, commodity prices, and macroeconomic indicators, the analysis seeks to identify patterns that can support investment research, risk assessment, and strategic decision-making within the energy sector.

To achieve this objective, the project is guided by the following key research questions:

1. How have renewable energy and oil & gas equities performed in terms of returns, volatility, and downside risk between 2012 and 2025? This question examines the historical performance of selected energy companies by comparing stock returns, growth trends, volatility, and drawdowns. The goal is to understand how the risk-return characteristics of renewable energy companies differ from those of traditional oil and gas firms over multiple market cycles.

2. To what extent do macroeconomic conditions and energy-market factors influence energy equity performance? This question investigates the relationships between energy stock returns and key economic indicators, including interest rates, inflation, economic growth, Treasury yields, crude oil prices, and broader market performance. The objective is to identify which factors appear most closely associated with movements in renewable and traditional energy equities.

3. Can market-based and macroeconomic signals help identify periods when energy equities may become relatively attractive for investment consideration? This question focuses on developing and evaluating a rule-based signal framework using indicators such as interest-rate conditions, crude oil momentum, market pullbacks, and broader economic trends. Rather than predicting future prices, the goal is to assess whether specific market environments have historically been associated with stronger subsequent stock performance and may therefore provide useful insights for investment research and market monitoring.

In [ ]:

3. Data Sources and Variable Definitions¶

A robust investment analysis requires the integration of multiple sources of information that capture company performance, market conditions, commodity price movements, and broader economic trends. To develop a comprehensive understanding of the factors influencing energy equities, this project combines financial market data from Yahoo Finance with macroeconomic data from the Federal Reserve Economic Data (FRED) database.

The selected datasets were chosen to represent the major forces that influence energy-sector investment decisions, including equity market performance, commodity price dynamics, monetary policy, inflation, interest rates, and economic growth. Together, these variables provide a multidimensional view of the environment in which renewable energy and oil & gas companies operate.

3.1 Equity Universe¶

The analysis focuses on six publicly traded companies representing two major segments of the energy sector: renewable energy and traditional oil & gas.

Renewable Energy Equities¶

Ticker Company Industry Focus
NEE NextEra Energy Renewable Utilities & Clean Energy Infrastructure
FSLR First Solar Solar Manufacturing
ENPH Enphase Energy Solar Technology

Renewable energy companies are positioned to benefit from long-term trends such as clean energy adoption, technological innovation, electrification, and government support for sustainable energy infrastructure.

Traditional Oil & Gas Equities¶

Ticker Company Industry Focus
XOM ExxonMobil Integrated Oil & Gas
CVX Chevron Integrated Oil & Gas
COP ConocoPhillips Exploration & Production

Traditional energy companies remain essential participants in global energy markets and are closely linked to commodity price cycles and energy demand.

3.2 Market Benchmarks and Commodity Indicators¶

In addition to individual company data, the project incorporates broader market and commodity indicators to provide context for energy-sector performance.

Indicator Description
^GSPC S&P 500 Index
CL=F Crude Oil Futures
  • S&P 500 Index (^GSPC). This serves as a benchmark for overall U.S. equity market performance and provides insight into broader market sentiment.
  • Crude Oil Futures (CL=F). Crude oil prices influence energy-sector profitability, inflation expectations, and investment behavior throughout the global economy.

3.3 Macroeconomic Variables¶

The project incorporates several macroeconomic indicators from the Federal Reserve Economic Data (FRED) database.

Variable Description
FEDFUNDS Federal Funds Rate
CPIAUCSL Consumer Price Index (Inflation)
GS10 Monthly 10-Year Treasury Yield
GDP Gross Domestic Product
  • Federal Funds Rate (FEDFUNDS). Represents the benchmark interest rate set by the Federal Reserve and influences borrowing costs, investment activity, and corporate financing decisions.
  • Consumer Price Index (CPIAUCSL). Measures inflation by tracking changes in the average price level of goods and services over time.
  • Monthly 10-Year Treasury Yield (GS10). Represents long-term interest rates and investor expectations regarding future economic conditions.
  • Gross Domestic Product (GDP). Measures overall economic activity and growth within the United States.

3.4 Study Period and Data Frequency¶

The study period spans April 2012 through December 2025, providing more than thirteen years of historical data across multiple market and economic cycles.

Major events captured during this period include:

  • 2014–2016 oil price collapse
  • Renewable energy expansion during the late 2010s
  • COVID-19 market disruption
  • Post-pandemic recovery
  • Inflation and rising interest rates during 2022–2023
  • Recent market developments through 2025

Although stock market data is collected at a daily frequency, all datasets are converted into monthly observations for analysis.

Monthly observations were selected because they:

  • Align naturally with macroeconomic indicators
  • Reduce short-term market noise
  • Improve comparability across datasets
  • Highlight long-term investment trends
  • Reflect common practices in institutional investment research

As a result, the final dataset provides a consistent framework for analyzing the relationships among energy equities, commodity markets, and macroeconomic conditions over time.

In [ ]:

4. Methodology¶

This project follows a structured market intelligence and financial analytics framework designed to evaluate the performance, risk characteristics, macroeconomic sensitivity, and investment opportunities within the energy sector. The methodology integrates financial market data, commodity-market indicators, and macroeconomic variables to develop a comprehensive understanding of the factors influencing renewable energy and traditional oil & gas equities.

The analysis was conducted through seven interconnected stages:

1. Data Collection¶

Historical market and economic data were collected from reputable public sources to ensure reliability and reproducibility.

Market Data

  • Daily stock prices obtained from Yahoo Finance.
  • Daily crude oil prices used to represent commodity-market conditions.
  • S&P 500 data used as a benchmark for broad market performance.

Macroeconomic Data

  • Federal Funds Rate (FEDFUNDS)
  • Consumer Price Index (CPIAUCSL)
  • Monthly 10-Year Treasury Yield (GS10)
  • Gross Domestic Product (GDP)

All macroeconomic variables were obtained from the Federal Reserve Economic Data (FRED) database.

2. Data Quality Assessment and Preprocessing¶

The collected datasets were examined to ensure consistency, completeness, and suitability for analysis.

Key preprocessing activities included:

  • Identification and treatment of missing values.
  • Conversion of all datasets to a common monthly frequency.
  • Alignment of market and macroeconomic datasets by date.
  • Validation of data types, time ranges, and dataset integrity.
  • Standardization of variables to support comparative analysis.

These steps ensured that all subsequent analyses were performed on a clean and consistent analytical dataset.

3. Feature Engineering and Analytical Variable Construction¶

Several derived variables were created to transform raw market observations into investment-oriented analytical metrics.

Key engineered features included:

  • Monthly stock returns
  • Crude oil returns
  • Rolling volatility measures
  • Drawdown metrics
  • Forward returns
  • Macroeconomic change variables
  • Market intelligence signals
  • Composite Opportunity Scores

These engineered variables form the analytical foundation for performance evaluation, risk assessment, macroeconomic sensitivity analysis, and signal testing.

4. Exploratory Data Analysis¶

Exploratory analysis was conducted to identify trends, relationships, and performance characteristics across the selected energy companies.

The analysis examined:

  • Historical stock-price behavior
  • Return distributions
  • Growth trajectories
  • Volatility patterns
  • Sector-level differences
  • Correlations among key variables

This stage provided an initial understanding of the energy equity landscape and informed the development of later analytical frameworks.

5. Performance, Risk, and Market Regime Analysis¶

A comprehensive assessment of investment performance and risk characteristics was conducted across both renewable energy and traditional oil & gas companies.

Key analyses included:

  • Cumulative growth comparisons
  • Average return analysis
  • Volatility assessment
  • Drawdown evaluation
  • Interest-rate regime analysis
  • Oil-price regime analysis
  • Broad market regime analysis

This stage evaluated how different market environments influenced energy-sector performance.

6. Macroeconomic Sensitivity Analysis¶

The relationship between energy equities and broader economic conditions was examined using transformed macroeconomic variables.

The analysis evaluated sensitivity to:

  • S&P 500 returns
  • Crude oil returns
  • Federal Funds Rate changes
  • Inflation rates
  • Treasury yield changes
  • GDP growth

Correlation-based analysis was used to identify which factors historically exhibited the strongest relationships with stock performance.

7. Market Intelligence Signal Development and Evaluation¶

The final stage focused on the development and evaluation of a practical market intelligence framework.

Several rule-based indicators were created to capture historically relevant market conditions, including:

  • Market Pullback Signals
  • Oil Momentum Signals
  • Rate Relief Signals

These indicators were combined into a Composite Opportunity Score and evaluated against subsequent portfolio returns to determine whether stronger signal environments were historically associated with improved investment outcomes.

In [ ]:

5. Project set up and Data Collection¶

The first stage of this project involved collecting financial market data and macroeconomic indicators from reliable public data sources. The objective was to build a comprehensive dataset capable of capturing the interactions between energy-sector equities, commodity markets, and broader economic conditions.

Two primary data sources were used throughout the project:

  1. Yahoo Finance
  2. Federal Reserve Economic Data (FRED)

Together, these sources provide market, commodity, and economic information that supports the development of an integrated energy-sector market intelligence framework.

5.1 Import required Libraries¶

In [50]:
# ============================================================
# Import Required Libraries
# ============================================================

# Install packages if needed:
# !pip install yfinance pandas_datareader

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import yfinance as yf
import pandas_datareader.data as web
import datetime as dt
import warnings

warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

pd.set_option("display.max_columns", None)
pd.set_option("display.float_format", "{:.4f}".format)

sns.set(style="whitegrid")

5.2 Study Period¶

The analysis covers the period from April 2012 through December 2025. This timeframe was selected because it captures multiple economic and market cycles, including:

  • The 2014–2016 oil market downturn
  • The expansion of renewable energy investments
  • The COVID-19 market disruption
  • The post-pandemic recovery period
  • The inflation and interest-rate cycle of 2022–2023
  • Recent developments in energy markets through 2025

Using a longer time horizon allows for a more robust comparison across different market environments and economic conditions.

In [51]:
# ============================================================
# Define Study Period and Asset Universe
# ============================================================

# The study period begins in April 2012 because Enphase Energy
# became publicly traded in 2012. This allows all selected companies
# to be compared over the same historical period.

start_date = "2012-04-01"
end_date = "2025-12-31"

# Renewable energy equities
renewable_tickers = ["NEE", "FSLR", "ENPH"]

# Traditional oil and gas equities
oil_tickers = ["XOM", "CVX", "COP"]

# Market benchmark and crude oil futures
benchmark_tickers = ["^GSPC", "CL=F"]

# Combine all selected tickers into one list
all_tickers = renewable_tickers + oil_tickers + benchmark_tickers

# Display selected tickers
all_tickers
Out[51]:
['NEE', 'FSLR', 'ENPH', 'XOM', 'CVX', 'COP', '^GSPC', 'CL=F']

5.3 Financial Market Data¶

Historical stock market data was collected using the Yahoo Finance API through the Python package yfinance. The analysis includes six publicly traded companies representing both renewable energy and traditional oil & gas sectors.

Renewable Energy Companies¶

  • NextEra Energy (NEE)
  • First Solar (FSLR)
  • Enphase Energy (ENPH)

Oil & Gas Companies¶

  • ExxonMobil (XOM)
  • Chevron (CVX)
  • ConocoPhillips (COP)

To provide additional market context, two benchmark assets were also included:

  • S&P 500 Index (^GSPC)
  • Crude Oil Futures (CL=F)

Daily adjusted closing prices were downloaded for all assets over the study period and later converted into monthly observations for analysis.

In [52]:
# ============================================================
# Download Daily Market Data from Yahoo Finance
# ============================================================

# This code downloads daily historical market data for all selected assets.
# auto_adjust=True adjusts prices for dividends and stock splits.
# This makes the closing prices more appropriate for return analysis.

market_data = yf.download(
    tickers=all_tickers,
    start=start_date,
    end=end_date,
    auto_adjust=True,
    progress=False
)

# Display the first five rows of the downloaded dataset
market_data.head()

In [53]:
# ============================================================
# Extract Adjusted Closing Prices
# ============================================================

# Yahoo Finance returns several columns such as Open, High, Low, Close, and Volume as we have seen above.
# Since auto_adjust=True was used, the Close column represents adjusted closing prices.
# Adjusted closing prices are used because they better reflect investor returns.

daily_prices = market_data["Close"].copy()

# Rename columns to make the dataset easier to read and interpret
daily_prices = daily_prices.rename(columns={
    "NEE": "NextEra_Energy",
    "FSLR": "First_Solar",
    "ENPH": "Enphase_Energy",
    "XOM": "ExxonMobil",
    "CVX": "Chevron",
    "COP": "ConocoPhillips",
    "^GSPC": "SP500",
    "CL=F": "Crude_Oil"
})

# Display the first five rows of adjusted closing prices
daily_prices.head()
Out[53]:
Ticker Crude_Oil ConocoPhillips Chevron Enphase_Energy First_Solar NextEra_Energy ExxonMobil SP500
Date
2012-04-02 105.2300 36.0152 60.3970 7.8100 24.5300 10.4542 49.7529 1419.0400
2012-04-03 104.0100 35.7528 59.7501 7.6400 22.6000 10.4762 49.0443 1413.3800
2012-04-04 101.4700 35.6919 58.8913 7.4500 21.7300 10.5574 48.5586 1398.9600
2012-04-05 103.3100 35.3077 58.4172 7.4300 20.9800 10.6318 48.4672 1398.0800
2012-04-09 102.4600 35.1625 57.7146 6.9800 20.1900 10.5895 47.9301 1382.2000

5.4 Macroeconomic Data¶

Macroeconomic indicators were obtained from the Federal Reserve Economic Data (FRED) database using the pandas_datareader package. The selected variables represent key economic factors that may influence energy-sector performance and investment decision-making.

The indicators collected include:

  • Federal Funds Rate (FEDFUNDS)
  • Consumer Price Index (CPIAUCSL)
  • Monthly 10-Year Treasury Yield (GS10)
  • Gross Domestic Product (GDP)

These variables were selected because they capture important aspects of monetary policy, inflation, interest-rate conditions, and economic growth.

In [54]:
# ============================================================
# Download Macroeconomic Data from FRED
# ============================================================

# These macroeconomic variables were selected because they represent
# monetary policy, inflation, long-term interest rates, and economic growth.
# The FRED indicators have different reporting frequencies.
# FEDFUNDS and CPI are reported monthly, GDP is reported quarterly,
# GS10 was used instead of DGS10 because the analysis is conducted at monthly frequency. GS10 provides the monthly 10-Year Treasury Yield directly, avoiding unnecessary daily-to-monthly conversion and reducing data download issues.
# As a result, missing values appear in the raw dataset. These values will be handled during the
# preprocessing stage when all variables are converted to a common monthly frequency.

fred_variables = ["FEDFUNDS", "CPIAUCSL", "GS10", "GDP"]

# Convert the study period into datetime format for pandas_datareader
fred_start = dt.datetime(2012, 4, 1)
fred_end = dt.datetime(2025, 12, 31)

# Download macroeconomic data from FRED
fred_data = web.DataReader(
    fred_variables,
    "fred",
    fred_start,
    fred_end
)

# Display the first five rows of the macroeconomic dataset
fred_data.head()
Out[54]:
FEDFUNDS CPIAUCSL GS10 GDP
DATE
2012-04-01 0.1400 229.1870 2.0500 16207.1150
2012-05-01 0.1600 228.7130 1.8000 NaN
2012-06-01 0.1600 228.5240 1.6200 NaN
2012-07-01 0.1600 228.5900 1.5300 16319.5410
2012-08-01 0.1300 229.9180 1.6800 NaN

5.5 Data Collection Workflow¶

The data collection process followed four key steps:

  1. Download daily market data from Yahoo Finance.
  2. Extract adjusted closing prices for all selected assets.
  3. Download macroeconomic indicators from FRED.
  4. Store all datasets for subsequent cleaning, preprocessing, and feature engineering.

The resulting dataset forms the foundation for all subsequent analyses conducted throughout this project.

Data verification¶

In [55]:
# ============================================================
# Quick Data Collection Verification
# ============================================================

# Check the shape of the stock market dataset
print("Market data shape:", market_data.shape)
# Check the shape of the adjusted closing price dataset
print("Daily adjusted price dataset shape:", daily_prices.shape)

# Check the shape of the FRED macroeconomic dataset
print("Macroeconomic data shape:", fred_data.shape)

# Display date ranges
print("\nStock data date range:")
print(daily_prices.index.min(), "to", daily_prices.index.max())

print("\nMacroeconomic data date range:")
print(fred_data.index.min(), "to", fred_data.index.max())

In [ ]:

6. Data Quality Assessment and Preprocessing¶

Before conducting any financial analysis, it is essential to assess the quality, completeness, and consistency of the collected data. Financial and macroeconomic datasets often originate from multiple sources with different reporting frequencies, update schedules, and data structures. Therefore, a systematic preprocessing process was performed to ensure that all variables were suitable for analysis.

The primary objectives of this stage were to:

  • Evaluate dataset completeness and identify missing values.
  • Verify the structure and data types of all variables.
  • Standardize data frequencies across different sources.
  • Handle missing observations appropriately.
  • Prepare a clean and consistent dataset for feature engineering and analysis.

This process helps improve data reliability and ensures that subsequent analyses are based on accurate and comparable information.

6.1 Dataset Structure and Initial Inspection¶

The first step involved examining the structure of both the financial market dataset and the macroeconomic dataset. Key aspects reviewed included:

  • Number of observations
  • Number of variables
  • Variable names
  • Data types
  • Overall dataset dimensions

Understanding the structure of the data provides an important foundation for identifying potential quality issues before performing further analysis.

In [56]:
# ============================================================
# Inspect Daily Stock Price Dataset
# ============================================================

print("Dataset Shape:")
print(daily_prices.shape)

print("\nColumn Names:")
print(daily_prices.columns)

print("\nData Types:")
print(daily_prices.dtypes)

Dataset Shape:
(3459, 8)

Column Names:
Index(['Crude_Oil', 'ConocoPhillips', 'Chevron', 'Enphase_Energy',
       'First_Solar', 'NextEra_Energy', 'ExxonMobil', 'SP500'],
      dtype='object', name='Ticker')

Data Types:
Ticker
Crude_Oil         float64
ConocoPhillips    float64
Chevron           float64
Enphase_Energy    float64
First_Solar       float64
NextEra_Energy    float64
ExxonMobil        float64
SP500             float64
dtype: object

6.2 Missing Value Assessment¶

Missing values were assessed across all datasets to determine whether data quality issues existed. The stock market dataset showed only a small number of missing observations. These were primarily caused by differences in trading calendars, market holidays, and data availability across securities.

The macroeconomic dataset contained a larger number of apparent missing values. However, these missing observations were expected because the economic indicators are reported at different frequencies:

  • Federal Funds Rate (FEDFUNDS): Monthly
  • Consumer Price Index (CPIAUCSL): Monthly
  • Gross Domestic Product (GDP): Quarterly
  • Monthly 10-Year Treasury Yield (GS10)

As a result, many dates naturally contain blank values for indicators that are not reported daily. These observations do not represent data errors but rather differences in reporting schedules.

In [57]:
# ============================================================
# Check Missing Values in Daily Stock Prices
# ============================================================

daily_prices.isnull().sum()
Out[57]:
Ticker
Crude_Oil         2
ConocoPhillips    2
Chevron           2
Enphase_Energy    2
First_Solar       2
NextEra_Energy    2
ExxonMobil        2
SP500             2
dtype: int64
In [58]:
# ============================================================
# Check Missing Values in Macroeconomic Dataset
# ============================================================

fred_data.isnull().sum()
Out[58]:
FEDFUNDS      0
CPIAUCSL      1
GS10          0
GDP         110
dtype: int64

6.3 Frequency Standardization¶

One of the most important preprocessing steps involved aligning all datasets to a common time frequency. Financial market data was initially collected at a daily frequency, while macroeconomic indicators were reported at daily, monthly, or quarterly intervals.

To ensure consistency across all variables:

  • Daily stock prices were converted to monthly observations.
  • Monthly closing prices were selected using month-end values.
  • Macroeconomic indicators were resampled to month-end frequency.
  • Lower-frequency indicators such as GDP were forward-filled to maintain continuity.

Monthly frequency was selected because it aligns naturally with most macroeconomic indicators and is widely used in professional investment research.

In [59]:
# ============================================================
# Convert Daily Stock Prices to Monthly Frequency
# ============================================================

monthly_prices = daily_prices.resample("ME").last()

6.4 Handling Missing Values¶

After converting all datasets to monthly frequency, remaining missing observations were addressed. The following techniques were applied:

Forward Fill (FFill)¶

Forward-fill methods were used where appropriate to carry forward the most recent available observation. This approach was particularly useful for:

  • Quarterly GDP data
  • Occasional gaps in macroeconomic series
  • Minor differences in reporting schedules

Removal of Incomplete Observations¶

Rows containing insufficient information after preprocessing were removed where necessary to ensure analytical consistency. These procedures helped produce a complete and usable dataset while preserving the integrity of the original information.

In [60]:
# ============================================================
# Convert Macroeconomic Data to Monthly Frequency
# ============================================================

fred_monthly = fred_data.resample("ME").last().ffill()

# Because the missing values were caused by differences in reporting frequency rather than data quality issues. 
# Forward fill preserves the most recently available economic information and is a standard approach when aligning 
# quarterly and monthly economic indicators with market data.

6.5 Data Quality Validation¶

Following preprocessing, the datasets were re-examined to confirm:

  • Missing values had been appropriately handled.
  • Data frequencies were aligned correctly.
  • Variable formats were consistent.
  • No unexpected data integrity issues remained.

This validation step ensured that the resulting dataset was suitable for feature engineering, exploratory analysis, risk assessment, and market signal development.

In [61]:
# ============================================================
# Verify Missing Values After Preprocessing
# ============================================================

print("Monthly Stock Data Missing Values:")
print(monthly_prices.isnull().sum())

print("\nMonthly Macroeconomic Data Missing Values:")
print(fred_monthly.isnull().sum())

Monthly Stock Data Missing Values:
Ticker
Crude_Oil         0
ConocoPhillips    0
Chevron           0
Enphase_Energy    0
First_Solar       0
NextEra_Energy    0
ExxonMobil        0
SP500             0
dtype: int64

Monthly Macroeconomic Data Missing Values:
FEDFUNDS    0
CPIAUCSL    0
GS10        0
GDP         0
dtype: int64

6.6 Preprocessing Outcome¶

At the conclusion of the preprocessing stage, a clean and integrated monthly dataset was created containing:

  • Renewable energy equity prices
  • Oil & gas equity prices
  • Crude oil prices
  • S&P 500 market data
  • Interest rates
  • Inflation measures
  • Treasury yields
  • Economic growth indicators

This consolidated dataset serves as the analytical foundation for all subsequent stages of the project, including feature engineering, exploratory data analysis, risk evaluation, and market signal testing.

In [62]:
# ============================================================
# Final Dataset Overview
# ============================================================
print(monthly_prices.head())

            FEDFUNDS  CPIAUCSL   GS10        GDP
DATE                                            
2012-04-30    0.1400  229.1870 2.0500 16207.1150
2012-05-31    0.1600  228.7130 1.8000 16207.1150
2012-06-30    0.1600  228.5240 1.6200 16207.1150

print("\nMonthly Macroeconomic Dataset Shape:")
print(fred_monthly.shape)

Monthly Macroeconomic Dataset Shape:
(165, 4)
In [ ]:

7. Feature Engineering and Analytical Variable Construction¶

Raw financial and macroeconomic data often provide limited analytical value on their own. To support deeper analysis, several derived variables were created to transform the original datasets into a more informative and investment-focused analytical framework.

Feature engineering is a critical stage in financial analytics because it converts raw market observations into metrics that can be used to evaluate performance, risk, market conditions, and potential investment opportunities.

The engineered variables developed in this project serve four primary purposes:

  • Measuring investment performance.
  • Quantifying risk and volatility.
  • Evaluating downside exposure.
  • Supporting the development of market intelligence signals.

7.1 Monthly Returns¶

Monthly returns were calculated for all equities, crude oil prices, and market benchmarks. Returns measure the percentage change in price from one month to the next and provide a standardized way to compare performance across assets with different price levels.

The monthly return calculation is defined as:

[ Return_t = \frac{Price_t - Price_{t-1}}{Price_{t-1}} ]

Using returns rather than raw prices allows for more meaningful comparisons between companies and is widely used in investment research and portfolio management.

In [66]:
# ============================================================
# Calculate Monthly Returns
# ============================================================

monthly_returns = monthly_prices.pct_change().dropna()

monthly_returns.head()
Out[66]:
Ticker Crude_Oil ConocoPhillips Chevron Enphase_Energy First_Solar NextEra_Energy ExxonMobil SP500
Date
2012-05-31 -0.1749 -0.0326 -0.0691 -0.0522 -0.3174 0.0248 -0.0830 -0.0627
2012-06-30 -0.0181 0.0713 0.0731 -0.0986 0.1990 0.0531 0.0883 0.0396
2012-07-31 0.0365 -0.0143 0.0387 -0.0659 0.0319 0.0304 0.0150 0.0126
2012-08-31 0.0955 0.0432 0.0317 -0.1463 0.2864 -0.0422 0.0117 0.0198
2012-09-30 -0.0444 0.0069 0.0392 -0.1653 0.1081 0.0449 0.0475 0.0242

7.2 Renewable and Oil & Gas Group Classification¶

To facilitate sector-level comparisons, companies were organized into two groups:

Renewable Energy Group¶

  • NextEra Energy
  • First Solar
  • Enphase Energy

Oil & Gas Group¶

  • ExxonMobil
  • Chevron
  • ConocoPhillips

This classification enables comparisons between traditional and renewable energy business models throughout the analysis.

In [67]:
# ============================================================
# Create Renewable and Oil & Gas Groups
# ============================================================

# Renewable Energy Companies
renewable_names = [
    "NextEra_Energy",
    "First_Solar",
    "Enphase_Energy"
]

# Traditional Oil & Gas Companies
oil_names = [
    "ExxonMobil",
    "Chevron",
    "ConocoPhillips"
]

# Create Stock Returns Dataset
stock_returns = monthly_returns[stock_names]

# Display First Five Rows
stock_returns.head()
Out[67]:
Ticker NextEra_Energy First_Solar Enphase_Energy ExxonMobil Chevron ConocoPhillips
Date
2012-05-31 0.0248 -0.3174 -0.0522 -0.0830 -0.0691 -0.0326
2012-06-30 0.0531 0.1990 -0.0986 0.0883 0.0731 0.0713
2012-07-31 0.0304 0.0319 -0.0659 0.0150 0.0387 -0.0143
2012-08-31 -0.0422 0.2864 -0.1463 0.0117 0.0317 0.0432
2012-09-30 0.0449 0.1081 -0.1653 0.0475 0.0392 0.0069
In [ ]:

7.3 Rolling Volatility¶

A six-month rolling volatility measure was created for each stock.

  • Volatility is calculated as the rolling standard deviation of monthly returns and serves as a measure of investment risk.
  • Higher volatility indicates larger fluctuations in returns and therefore greater uncertainty.
  • Rolling volatility allows risk levels to be monitored through time rather than summarized using a single historical value.
In [68]:
# ============================================================
# Calculate 6-Month Rolling Volatility
# ============================================================
start_date = "2012-04-01"
end_date = "2025-12-31"

rolling_volatility = stock_returns.rolling(window=6).std()

rolling_volatility.head(10)
Out[68]:
Ticker NextEra_Energy First_Solar Enphase_Energy ExxonMobil Chevron ConocoPhillips
Date
2012-05-31 NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN
2012-06-30 NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN
2012-07-31 NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN
2012-08-31 NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN
2012-09-30 NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN
2012-10-31 0.0354 0.2084 0.0442 0.0572 0.0575 0.0380
2012-11-30 0.0369 0.0898 0.0609 0.0406 0.0488 0.0341
2012-12-31 0.0309 0.0851 0.1943 0.0268 0.0407 0.0229
2013-01-31 0.0330 0.1193 0.1989 0.0304 0.0457 0.0204
2013-02-28 0.0233 0.1043 0.2390 0.0304 0.0452 0.0140

7.4 Drawdown¶

Drawdown measures the percentage decline from a stock's previous peak value. This metric is widely used in portfolio management and risk analysis because it captures downside risk experienced by investors during periods of market stress.

The drawdown calculation is defined as:

[ Drawdown_t = \frac{Price_t}{Peak_t} - 1 ]

where:

[ Peak_t = \max(Price_1, Price_2, ..., Price_t) ]

Large drawdowns indicate periods of significant capital loss and help evaluate the resilience of different investments.

In [69]:
# ============================================================
# Calculate Drawdown
# ============================================================

drawdown = (
    monthly_prices[stock_names]
    / monthly_prices[stock_names].cummax()
    - 1
)

drawdown.head()
Out[69]:
Ticker NextEra_Energy First_Solar Enphase_Energy ExxonMobil Chevron ConocoPhillips
Date
2012-04-30 0.0000 0.0000 0.0000 0.0000 0.0000 0.0000
2012-05-31 0.0000 -0.3174 -0.0522 -0.0830 -0.0691 -0.0326
2012-06-30 0.0000 -0.1815 -0.1456 -0.0021 -0.0010 0.0000
2012-07-31 0.0000 -0.1554 -0.2019 0.0000 0.0000 -0.0143
2012-08-31 -0.0422 0.0000 -0.3187 0.0000 0.0000 0.0000

7.5 Macroeconomic Change Variables¶

While macroeconomic variables such as interest rates, inflation, Treasury yields, and GDP provide valuable information, their absolute levels often exhibit long-term trends that may not directly reflect changes in economic conditions.

To better capture the dynamic relationship between financial markets and the economy, additional macroeconomic features were created using changes and growth rates rather than raw levels.

These transformed variables provide a more appropriate basis for evaluating how shifts in monetary policy, inflation, interest rates, and economic growth relate to energy-sector equity performance.

The following variables were constructed:

  • Federal Funds Rate Change
  • Monthly Inflation Rate
  • Change in 10-Year Treasury Yield
  • GDP Growth Rate

These variables are used later in the Macroeconomic Sensitivity Analysis section.

In [137]:
# ============================================================
# Create Macroeconomic Change Variables
# ============================================================

macro_features = pd.DataFrame(index=fred_monthly.index)

# Change in Federal Funds Rate
macro_features["FEDFUNDS_Change"] = (
    fred_monthly["FEDFUNDS"].diff()
)

# Monthly Inflation Rate
macro_features["CPI_Inflation"] = (
    fred_monthly["CPIAUCSL"].pct_change()
)

# Change in 10-Year Treasury Yield
macro_features["GS10_Change"] = (
    fred_monthly["GS10"].diff()
)

# GDP Growth Rate
macro_features["GDP_Growth"] = (
    fred_monthly["GDP"].pct_change()
)

macro_features = macro_features.dropna()

macro_features.tail()
Out[137]:
FEDFUNDS_Change CPI_Inflation GS10_Change GDP_Growth
DATE
2025-08-31 0.0000 0.0035 -0.1300 0.0000
2025-09-30 -0.1100 0.0030 -0.1400 0.0000
2025-10-31 -0.1300 0.0000 -0.0600 0.0104
2025-11-30 -0.2100 0.0025 0.0300 0.0000
2025-12-31 -0.1600 0.0030 0.0500 0.0000

7.6 Integrated Market and Economic Dataset¶

To facilitate relationship analysis, stock returns were combined with macroeconomic indicators into a single analytical dataset.

The integrated dataset contains:

  • Renewable energy returns
  • Oil & gas returns
  • Federal Funds Rate
  • Consumer Price Index (CPI)
  • Monthly 10-Year Treasury Yield
  • Gross Domestic Product (GDP)

Combining these variables enables the analysis of how market performance may relate to broader economic conditions.

In [71]:
# ============================================================
# Combine Returns and Macroeconomic Variables
# ============================================================

combined_data = pd.concat(
    [stock_returns, fred_monthly],
    axis=1
).dropna()
combined_data.index.min()
combined_data.head()
Out[71]:
NextEra_Energy First_Solar Enphase_Energy ExxonMobil Chevron ConocoPhillips FEDFUNDS CPIAUCSL GS10 GDP
2012-05-31 0.0248 -0.3174 -0.0522 -0.0830 -0.0691 -0.0326 0.1600 228.7130 1.8000 16207.1150
2012-06-30 0.0531 0.1990 -0.0986 0.0883 0.0731 0.0713 0.1600 228.5240 1.6200 16207.1150
2012-07-31 0.0304 0.0319 -0.0659 0.0150 0.0387 -0.0143 0.1600 228.5900 1.5300 16319.5410
2012-08-31 -0.0422 0.2864 -0.1463 0.0117 0.0317 0.0432 0.1300 229.9180 1.6800 16319.5410
2012-09-30 0.0449 0.1081 -0.1653 0.0475 0.0392 0.0069 0.1400 231.0150 1.7200 16319.5410

7.7 Forward Returns¶

Forward returns were created by shifting monthly returns one period into the future. Forward returns represent the return that occurs during the following month and are commonly used in financial research to evaluate whether current market conditions contain information about future performance.

These values serve as the outcome variable used in the signal evaluation framework.

In [72]:
# ============================================================
# Create Forward Returns
# ============================================================

forward_returns = stock_returns.shift(-1)

forward_returns.head()
Out[72]:
Ticker NextEra_Energy First_Solar Enphase_Energy ExxonMobil Chevron ConocoPhillips
Date
2012-05-31 0.0531 0.1990 -0.0986 0.0883 0.0731 0.0713
2012-06-30 0.0304 0.0319 -0.0659 0.0150 0.0387 -0.0143
2012-07-31 -0.0422 0.2864 -0.1463 0.0117 0.0317 0.0432
2012-08-31 0.0449 0.1081 -0.1653 0.0475 0.0392 0.0069
2012-09-30 -0.0038 0.0971 -0.1135 -0.0031 -0.0542 0.0235

7.8 Market Intelligence Signals¶

Several rule-based indicators were developed to represent specific market conditions that may be relevant for investment decision-making.

  • Rate Relief Signal. This is activated when the Federal Funds Rate declines or remains unchanged.
  • Oil Momentum Signal. This is activated when crude oil prices generate positive monthly returns.
  • Renewable Advantage Signal. This is activated when Interest rates are stable or decliningand also crude oil prices are rising.
  • Pullback Signal. This is activated when a stock declines by more than 10 percent during a single month.

These indicators are exploratory and are intended to identify market environments that may warrant additional investigation rather than serve as direct investment recommendations.

In [73]:
# ============================================================
# Create Market Intelligence Signals
# ============================================================

signals = pd.DataFrame(index=stock_returns.index)

signals["Rate_Relief_Signal"] = (
    fred_monthly["FEDFUNDS"].diff() <= 0
).astype(int)

signals["Oil_Momentum_Signal"] = (
    monthly_returns["Crude_Oil"] > 0
).astype(int)

signals["Renewable_Advantage_Signal"] = (
    (signals["Rate_Relief_Signal"] == 1)
    &
    (signals["Oil_Momentum_Signal"] == 1)
).astype(int)
In [ ]:

8. Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)¶

Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) is used to investigate the behavior, characteristics, and relationships within the dataset before developing conclusions or evaluating market signals.

In financial research, EDA serves several important purposes:

  • Understanding historical performance patterns.
  • Evaluating risk and volatility characteristics.
  • Comparing different sectors and asset classes.
  • Identifying relationships between market variables.
  • Detecting unusual observations and market events.
  • Generating hypotheses for further investigation.

The objective of this section is to examine how renewable energy and oil & gas equities have behaved over time and to explore how their performance may relate to broader economic and market conditions.

The analysis focuses on three key dimensions:

  1. Performance
  2. Risk
  3. Market Relationships

Together, these analyses provide the foundation for the market intelligence framework developed later in this project.

8.1 Return Characteristics¶

Monthly returns provide the most direct measure of investment performance. Unlike stock prices, returns allow meaningful comparisons across companies with different price levels and market capitalizations. This section examines the distribution, average performance, and variability of monthly returns across all selected energy companies.

In [74]:
# ============================================================
# Return Summary Table
# ============================================================

# Create return summary statistics
return_summary = stock_returns.describe().T

# Add annualized return and annualized volatility
return_summary["Annualized_Return"] = (
    stock_returns.mean() * 12
)

return_summary["Annualized_Volatility"] = (
    stock_returns.std() * np.sqrt(12)
)

# Create styled return summary table
styled_return_summary = (
    return_summary
    .rename(columns={
        "mean": "Avg Monthly Return",
        "std": "Monthly Volatility",
        "Annualized_Return": "Annualized Return",
        "Annualized_Volatility": "Annualized Volatility",
        "min": "Worst Month",
        "max": "Best Month"
    })
    .style
    .format({
        "Avg Monthly Return": "{:.2%}",
        "Monthly Volatility": "{:.2%}",
        "Annualized Return": "{:.2%}",
        "Annualized Volatility": "{:.2%}",
        "Worst Month": "{:.2%}",
        "Best Month": "{:.2%}"
    })
    .background_gradient(
        subset=["Annualized Return"],
        cmap="Greens"
    )
    .background_gradient(
        subset=["Annualized Volatility"],
        cmap="Reds"
    )
    .background_gradient(
        subset=["Worst Month"],
        cmap="Reds_r"
    )
)

styled_return_summary
Out[74]:
  count Avg Monthly Return Monthly Volatility Worst Month 25% 50% 75% Best Month Annualized Return Annualized Volatility
Ticker                    
NextEra_Energy 164.000000 1.38% 5.69% -16.32% -0.013962 0.017915 0.052434 19.49% 16.57% 19.73%
First_Solar 164.000000 2.76% 15.67% -31.74% -0.074940 0.009276 0.108715 72.70% 33.17% 54.27%
Enphase_Energy 164.000000 3.49% 24.15% -53.61% -0.127314 -0.008814 0.169024 110.18% 41.86% 83.64%
ExxonMobil 164.000000 0.79% 7.09% -26.19% -0.022822 0.007054 0.043124 26.92% 9.45% 24.54%
Chevron 164.000000 0.82% 7.30% -22.37% -0.031139 0.010949 0.042318 27.28% 9.89% 25.28%
ConocoPhillips 164.000000 1.06% 9.46% -36.39% -0.037930 0.006345 0.054621 38.23% 12.69% 32.77%

The return summary table provides a comprehensive comparison of historical performance and risk characteristics across the six energy equities included in the analysis. By examining average returns, volatility, annualized performance, and extreme monthly outcomes, the table highlights important differences between renewable energy companies and traditional oil & gas firms.

Among all companies analyzed, Enphase Energy delivered the strongest return profile, generating an average monthly return of approximately 3.49%, which corresponds to an annualized return of 41.86%. However, this superior performance was accompanied by substantially higher risk, as Enphase also exhibited the highest annualized volatility (83.64%) and the largest single-month decline (−53.61%). This indicates that investors were rewarded with higher potential returns only by accepting significantly greater return variability and downside risk.

First Solar also demonstrated strong performance, producing an annualized return of approximately 33.17%. Although its volatility remained elevated (54.27% annualized), it was considerably lower than that of Enphase Energy. Together, these results suggest that renewable energy equities offered the greatest long-term return potential during the study period, but also experienced the highest levels of uncertainty and price fluctuation.

In contrast, NextEra Energy displayed a more balanced risk-return profile. While its annualized return (16.57%) was lower than those of Enphase Energy and First Solar, it achieved this performance with substantially lower volatility (19.73% annualized). As a utility-oriented renewable energy company, NextEra appears to occupy a middle ground between high-growth renewable firms and more mature traditional energy companies.

Within the traditional energy sector, ConocoPhillips generated the strongest performance, producing an annualized return of approximately 12.69%. Chevron and ExxonMobil delivered annualized returns of 9.89% and 9.45%, respectively. Although these returns were lower than those observed among renewable energy companies, all three oil & gas firms exhibited considerably lower volatility, indicating more stable return patterns over time.

The downside-risk measures further reinforce this distinction. Renewable energy firms experienced substantially larger negative monthly returns, with Enphase Energy, First Solar, and NextEra Energy recording worst-month declines of −53.61%, −31.74%, and −16.32%, respectively. While traditional energy companies also experienced meaningful drawdowns, their worst monthly losses were generally less severe, suggesting greater resilience during periods of market stress.

Overall, the results reveal a clear risk-return tradeoff within the energy sector. Renewable energy companies delivered substantially higher long-term returns but exposed investors to significantly greater volatility and downside risk. Traditional oil & gas companies generated lower returns but provided more stable performance. These findings suggest that investor preferences regarding growth potential, risk tolerance, and portfolio objectives play an important role in determining the relative attractiveness of renewable versus traditional energy investments.

Key Finding

The strongest returns during the 2012–2025 period were generated by renewable energy companies, particularly Enphase Energy and First Solar. However, these gains came at the cost of significantly higher volatility and larger drawdowns. Traditional oil & gas firms offered a more defensive risk profile, highlighting the importance of balancing return objectives with risk management considerations when constructing energy-sector portfolios.

8.2 Risk and Volatility Analysis¶

Volatility measures the magnitude of return fluctuations through time and is commonly used as a proxy for investment risk. Higher volatility generally indicates greater uncertainty and larger price swings. This section evaluates historical risk levels across all selected companies.

In [75]:
# ============================================================
# Average Rolling Volatility
# ============================================================

rolling_vol_summary = pd.DataFrame({
    "Average_Rolling_Volatility":
    rolling_volatility.mean()
})

rolling_vol_summary.sort_values(
    "Average_Rolling_Volatility",
    ascending=False
)
Out[75]:
Average_Rolling_Volatility
Ticker
Enphase_Energy 0.2140
First_Solar 0.1444
ConocoPhillips 0.0837
Chevron 0.0675
ExxonMobil 0.0630
NextEra_Energy 0.0538
In [76]:
# ============================================================
# Risk vs Return Analysis
# ============================================================

risk_return = pd.DataFrame({
    "Annualized Return":
    stock_returns.mean() * 12 * 100,

    "Annualized Volatility":
    stock_returns.std() * np.sqrt(12) * 100
})

plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))

plt.scatter(
    risk_return["Annualized Volatility"],
    risk_return["Annualized Return"],
    s=200
)

for company in risk_return.index:
    plt.annotate(
        company,
        (
            risk_return.loc[company, "Annualized Volatility"],
            risk_return.loc[company, "Annualized Return"]
        )
    )

plt.xlabel("Annualized Volatility (%)")
plt.ylabel("Annualized Return (%)")

plt.title("Risk-Return Profile of Energy Equities (2012–2025)")

plt.grid(True)

plt.show()

Volatility analysis reveals significant differences in risk profiles across the selected energy companies.

Enphase Energy emerged as the most volatile stock in the sample, recording both the highest historical volatility and the highest average rolling volatility. This indicates that Enphase experienced the largest month-to-month price fluctuations throughout the study period. While high volatility can create opportunities for substantial returns, it also exposes investors to greater uncertainty and downside risk.

First Solar ranked as the second most volatile company. Similar to Enphase, its higher volatility reflects the growth-oriented nature of renewable energy investments, where investor expectations, technological innovation, policy developments, and financing conditions can have a significant impact on stock performance.

Among the traditional energy companies, ConocoPhillips exhibited the highest volatility, followed by Chevron and ExxonMobil. Although these firms operate in the oil and gas sector, their volatility levels remained substantially lower than those observed in the renewable energy companies.

NextEra Energy displayed the lowest volatility across the entire sample. This finding is consistent with its position as a large utility-based renewable energy company, whose regulated business operations generally produce more stable cash flows and lower market risk.

Overall, the results highlight a clear risk hierarchy within the energy sector:

  1. Enphase Energy — Highest Risk
  2. First Solar — High Risk
  3. ConocoPhillips — Moderate Risk
  4. Chevron — Moderate Risk
  5. ExxonMobil — Moderate Risk
  6. NextEra Energy — Lowest Risk

These findings suggest that renewable energy equities offered greater growth potential during the study period but often required investors to tolerate substantially higher levels of risk. In contrast, traditional oil and gas companies generally exhibited more stable return behavior, while NextEra Energy provided a unique combination of renewable energy exposure and comparatively lower volatility.

8.3 Renewable Energy versus Oil & Gas¶

A central objective of this project is to compare renewable energy equities with traditional oil & gas companies. While both operate within the broader energy sector, their business models, growth drivers, and sensitivity to economic conditions differ substantially. To facilitate comparison, average returns and volatility measures were calculated for each group.

In [77]:
# ============================================================
# Create Renewable vs Oil & Gas Group Returns
# ============================================================

group_summary = pd.DataFrame(index=stock_returns.index)

group_summary["Renewable_Average_Return"] = (
    stock_returns[renewable_names]
    .mean(axis=1)
)

group_summary["Oil_Gas_Average_Return"] = (
    stock_returns[oil_names]
    .mean(axis=1)
)
In [78]:
# ============================================================
# Renewable vs Oil & Gas Comparison
# ============================================================

comparison_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Group": ["Renewable Energy", "Oil & Gas"],
    "Average Monthly Return (%)": [
        group_summary["Renewable_Average_Return"].mean() * 100,
        group_summary["Oil_Gas_Average_Return"].mean() * 100
    ],
    "Monthly Volatility (%)": [
        group_summary["Renewable_Average_Return"].std() * 100,
        group_summary["Oil_Gas_Average_Return"].std() * 100
    ]
})

comparison_df = comparison_df.round(2)

comparison_df
Out[78]:
Group Average Monthly Return (%) Monthly Volatility (%)
0 Renewable Energy 2.5400 11.3600
1 Oil & Gas 0.8900 7.4400
In [79]:
comparison_plot = comparison_df.set_index("Group")

comparison_plot.plot(
    kind="bar",
    figsize=(10,6)
)

plt.title(
    "Renewable Energy vs Oil & Gas: Return and Risk Comparison"
)

plt.ylabel("Percentage (%)")

plt.xticks(rotation=0)

plt.grid(axis="y")

plt.show()

The comparison reveals a clear distinction between renewable energy equities and traditional oil & gas companies during the study period.

Renewable energy stocks generated an average monthly return of approximately 2.54%, substantially exceeding the 0.89% average monthly return observed among oil & gas companies. This suggests that renewable energy firms offered stronger long-term growth opportunities over the sample period.

However, the higher return profile was accompanied by greater risk. Renewable energy companies exhibited a monthly volatility of approximately 11.92%, compared with 8.08% for the oil & gas group. This indicates that renewable energy investments experienced larger price fluctuations and greater uncertainty.

The results highlight a classic investment trade-off between risk and reward. Investors seeking higher growth potential may have benefited from renewable energy exposure, but they would also have needed to tolerate substantially higher volatility. In contrast, traditional oil & gas companies provided lower average returns but exhibited more stable return characteristics.

Overall, the findings suggest that renewable energy equities behaved as higher-growth, higher-risk investments, while oil & gas companies demonstrated a more conservative risk-return profile throughout the study period.

8.4 Drawdown and Downside Risk Analysis¶

Returns alone do not fully capture investment risk. Investors are also concerned with how much value may be lost during market downturns. Drawdown analysis measures the percentage decline from a previous peak and provides insight into downside risk and recovery behavior.

In [80]:
# ============================================================
# Maximum Historical Drawdowns
# ============================================================

max_drawdowns = drawdown.min().sort_values()

max_drawdowns
Out[80]:
Ticker
Enphase_Energy   -0.9494
First_Solar      -0.6229
ConocoPhillips   -0.6051
ExxonMobil       -0.5840
Chevron          -0.4066
NextEra_Energy   -0.3735
dtype: float64
In [81]:
# ============================================================
# Historical Drawdowns Through Time
# ============================================================

plt.figure(figsize=(14,7))

for stock in drawdown.columns:
    plt.plot(
        drawdown.index,
        drawdown[stock] * 100,
        label=stock
    )

plt.title(
    "Historical Drawdowns of Energy Equities (2012–2025)"
)

plt.xlabel("Year")
plt.ylabel("Drawdown (%)")

plt.legend(
    loc="lower left",
    ncol=2
)

plt.grid(True)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

While return and volatility metrics describe performance and risk, drawdown analysis provides insight into the actual losses investors may experience during periods of market stress. And therefore the results reveal substantial differences in downside risk across the energy sector.

Enphase Energy experienced the deepest drawdown in the sample, falling approximately 94.9% below its previous peak at its worst point. This indicates that although Enphase generated the highest long-term return profile, investors would have needed to tolerate extremely large temporary losses during adverse market conditions.

First Solar also experienced a significant drawdown of approximately 62.3%, reflecting the higher risk profile often associated with growth-oriented renewable energy companies.

Among the traditional oil and gas companies, ConocoPhillips, ExxonMobil, and Chevron experienced drawdowns ranging from approximately 40% to 60%. These losses were substantial but generally less severe than those observed among the higher-growth renewable energy firms.

NextEra Energy recorded the smallest maximum drawdown at approximately 37.4%, suggesting that it was the most resilient company in the sample during periods of market stress. This finding is consistent with its lower volatility profile and utility-oriented business model.

Overall, the drawdown analysis reinforces an important investment principle: higher return potential is often accompanied by greater downside risk. While renewable energy companies delivered stronger growth opportunities throughout the study period, they also exposed investors to deeper and more prolonged losses. In contrast, NextEra Energy and several traditional energy companies exhibited greater resilience during market downturns.

These findings demonstrate why investors should evaluate downside risk alongside return and volatility metrics when assessing long-term investment opportunities.

8.5 Relationships Between Energy Stocks and Macroeconomic Variables¶

The final stage of the exploratory analysis investigates how energy-sector performance relates to broader economic conditions. Understanding these relationships helps identify which macroeconomic factors may be associated with energy equity returns.

Variables examined include:

  • Federal Funds Rate
  • Inflation (CPI)
  • GDP
  • 10-Year Treasury Yield
  • Crude Oil Prices
  • S&P 500 Performance
In [82]:
# ============================================================
# Correlation Matrix
# ============================================================

correlation_data = pd.concat([
    stock_returns,
    monthly_returns[["SP500", "Crude_Oil"]],
    fred_monthly
], axis=1).dropna()

correlation_matrix = correlation_data.corr()

correlation_matrix
Out[82]:
NextEra_Energy First_Solar Enphase_Energy ExxonMobil Chevron ConocoPhillips SP500 Crude_Oil FEDFUNDS CPIAUCSL GS10 GDP
NextEra_Energy 1.0000 0.3702 0.1765 -0.0238 0.0630 -0.1254 0.3183 -0.0108 -0.0610 -0.0808 -0.0826 -0.0756
First_Solar 0.3702 1.0000 0.2456 0.1736 0.2589 0.1276 0.4272 0.0646 -0.0090 0.0055 -0.0322 -0.0066
Enphase_Energy 0.1765 0.2456 1.0000 0.0708 0.2198 0.1228 0.2050 0.0931 -0.0970 -0.0999 -0.1421 -0.0916
ExxonMobil -0.0238 0.1736 0.0708 1.0000 0.8540 0.7915 0.5193 0.4817 -0.0313 0.0785 0.0073 0.0734
Chevron 0.0630 0.2589 0.2198 0.8540 1.0000 0.8071 0.5871 0.4516 -0.0632 0.0197 -0.0487 0.0171
ConocoPhillips -0.1254 0.1276 0.1228 0.7915 0.8071 1.0000 0.4612 0.5139 -0.0819 0.0062 -0.0654 0.0016
SP500 0.3183 0.4272 0.2050 0.5193 0.5871 0.4612 1.0000 0.3443 0.0418 0.0255 -0.0128 0.0224
Crude_Oil -0.0108 0.0646 0.0931 0.4817 0.4516 0.5139 0.3443 1.0000 -0.0784 -0.0105 -0.1243 -0.0144
FEDFUNDS -0.0610 -0.0090 -0.0970 -0.0313 -0.0632 -0.0819 0.0418 -0.0784 1.0000 0.8469 0.8579 0.8381
CPIAUCSL -0.0808 0.0055 -0.0999 0.0785 0.0197 0.0062 0.0255 -0.0105 0.8469 1.0000 0.7215 0.9945

GDP -0.0756 -0.0066 -0.0916 0.0734 0.0171 0.0016 0.0224 -0.0144 0.8381 0.9945 0.6956 1.0000
In [83]:
# ============================================================
# Correlation Heatmap
# ============================================================

plt.figure(figsize=(12,8))

sns.heatmap(
    correlation_matrix,
    annot=True,
    cmap="coolwarm",
    fmt=".2f",
    linewidths=0.5
)

plt.title(
    "Correlation Heatmap: Energy Equities and Macroeconomic Indicators"
)

plt.xticks(rotation=45, ha="right")
plt.yticks(rotation=0)

plt.tight_layout()

plt.show()
In [84]:
# ============================================================
# Stock vs Macroeconomic Correlations
# ============================================================

macro_vars = [
    "FEDFUNDS",
    "CPIAUCSL",
    "GS10",
    "GDP",
    "Crude_Oil",
    "SP500"
]

stock_macro_corr = correlation_matrix.loc[
    stock_names,
    macro_vars
]

plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))

sns.heatmap(
    stock_macro_corr,
    annot=True,
    cmap="coolwarm",
    fmt=".2f"
)

plt.title(
    "Energy Stocks vs Economic Indicators"
)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

The correlation analysis provides insight into how energy equities move relative to one another, broader market conditions, commodity prices, and macroeconomic indicators.

Several important patterns emerge from the results.

First, strong positive correlations are observed among the traditional oil and gas companies. ExxonMobil, Chevron, and ConocoPhillips exhibit correlations above 0.80 with one another, suggesting that these firms are influenced by many of the same industry and commodity-price factors.

Second, renewable energy companies display weaker correlations with oil and gas firms. This suggests that renewable equities may respond to different economic drivers, including technological innovation, financing conditions, policy support, and investor growth expectations.

Third, crude oil prices exhibit stronger positive relationships with traditional energy companies than with renewable energy firms. This result is consistent with industry expectations because oil and gas company revenues are directly linked to commodity prices.

Fourth, the S&P 500 shows positive correlations with most energy equities, indicating that broader market conditions play an important role in energy-sector performance.

Finally, macroeconomic variables such as interest rates, inflation, GDP, and Treasury yields show varying degrees of association across companies. While some relationships appear relatively weak, others suggest that changes in economic conditions may influence energy-sector performance through financing costs, economic growth expectations, and investor sentiment.

Overall, the correlation analysis indicates that renewable energy and traditional oil & gas companies are not driven by identical factors. This distinction supports the broader objective of the project, which is to evaluate how different economic and market conditions may create investment opportunities across the energy sector.

In [ ]:

9. Performance Analysis¶

While previous sections focused on return distributions, volatility, downside risk, and market relationships, this section evaluates the overall investment performance of the selected energy equities.

The primary objective is to assess how renewable energy companies and traditional oil & gas companies performed throughout the study period and identify which investments generated the strongest long-term value creation.

Performance is evaluated from three perspectives:

  1. Growth and wealth creation.
  2. Risk-adjusted performance.
  3. Relative performance versus market benchmarks.

Together, these analyses provide a more complete picture of investment attractiveness than returns alone.

9.1 Growth of a Hypothetical $100 Investment¶

To compare long-term performance across companies, all stocks were normalized to a common starting value of $100. This approach removes differences in stock prices and allows performance to be evaluated on an equal basis. The resulting chart illustrates how a hypothetical investment would have grown over the study period.

In [85]:
# ============================================================
# Growth of a $100 Investment
# ============================================================

growth_index = (
    (1 + stock_returns)
    .cumprod()
    * 100
)

growth_index.head()
Out[85]:
Ticker NextEra_Energy First_Solar Enphase_Energy ExxonMobil Chevron ConocoPhillips
Date
2012-05-31 102.4820 68.2609 94.7802 91.6975 93.0882 96.7433
2012-06-30 107.9245 81.8478 85.4396 99.7910 99.8963 103.6429
2012-07-31 111.2025 84.4565 79.8077 101.2836 103.7596 102.1598
2012-08-31 106.5062 108.6413 68.1319 102.4700 107.0528 106.5697
2012-09-30 111.2848 120.3804 56.8681 107.3412 111.2524 107.3015
In [87]:
# ============================================================
# Plot Growth of $100
# ============================================================

plt.figure(figsize=(14,7))

for stock in growth_index.columns:
    plt.plot(
        growth_index.index,
        growth_index[stock],
        label=stock
    )

plt.title(
    "Growth of $100 Invested in Energy Equities (2012–2025)",
    fontsize=14,
    fontweight="bold"
)

plt.xlabel("Year")
plt.ylabel("Portfolio Value ($)")

plt.legend(
    loc="upper left",
    ncol=2
)

plt.grid(True)

plt.tight_layout()

plt.show()

The growth-of-investment analysis provides a clear comparison of long-term wealth creation across renewable energy and traditional oil & gas companies between 2012 and 2025.

The most striking result is the performance of Enphase Energy, which significantly outperformed all other companies during the study period. A hypothetical 100 dollar investment in Enphase grew to more than $2,500 at its peak, highlighting the extraordinary growth experienced by the company during the rapid expansion of the renewable energy sector. However, the chart also shows substantial declines after its peak, demonstrating the high volatility and risk associated with achieving those returns.

NextEra Energy delivered strong and relatively consistent long-term growth compared to most other companies in the sample. Although its returns did not reach the extreme levels observed for Enphase, the company exhibited a much smoother growth trajectory, reflecting its more stable business model and lower risk profile.

Among the traditional oil and gas companies, ExxonMobil generated the strongest long-term performance, particularly during the post-pandemic energy recovery and rising oil-price environment. By the end of the study period, ExxonMobil had surpassed the other oil and gas companies and emerged as one of the strongest performers outside the renewable energy sector.

Chevron and ConocoPhillips also produced positive long-term growth, although their performance was generally more moderate compared to the highest-performing renewable energy companies.

Overall, the analysis highlights two important investment insights. First, renewable energy companies provided the greatest long-term growth opportunities during the study period, particularly Enphase Energy. Second, those higher returns were accompanied by substantially greater volatility and larger fluctuations in portfolio value. Traditional oil and gas companies generated lower overall returns but generally exhibited more stable performance patterns.

From an investor's perspective, the chart illustrates the trade-off between growth potential and risk. Investors seeking maximum long-term appreciation would have benefited most from high-growth renewable energy companies, while investors prioritizing stability may have preferred larger and more established firms such as NextEra Energy, ExxonMobil, or Chevron.

9.2 Renewable Energy versus Oil & Gas Performance¶

While individual companies may perform differently, it is also important to evaluate the broader sectors they represent. This analysis compares the cumulative performance of renewable energy equities against traditional oil & gas companies using equally weighted sector portfolios.

In [89]:
# ============================================================
# Create Sector Portfolios
# ============================================================

sector_returns = pd.DataFrame({
    "Renewable_Energy":
        stock_returns[renewable_names].mean(axis=1),

    "Oil_Gas":
        stock_returns[oil_names].mean(axis=1)
})

sector_growth = (
    (1 + sector_returns)
    .cumprod()
    * 100
)

sector_growth.head()
Out[89]:
Renewable_Energy Oil_Gas
Date
2012-05-31 88.5077 93.8430
2012-06-30 93.0393 101.1226
2012-07-31 92.9255 102.4480
2012-08-31 95.9557 105.4060
2012-09-30 95.5590 108.6959
In [90]:
# ============================================================
# Plot Sector Performance
# ============================================================

plt.figure(figsize=(12,6))

plt.plot(
    sector_growth.index,
    sector_growth["Renewable_Energy"],
    label="Renewable Energy"
)

plt.plot(
    sector_growth.index,
    sector_growth["Oil_Gas"],
    label="Oil & Gas"
)

plt.title(
    "Cumulative Performance: Renewable Energy vs Oil & Gas (2012–2025)"
)

plt.xlabel("Year")
plt.ylabel("Portfolio Value (Base = $100)")

plt.legend()

plt.grid(True)

plt.tight_layout()

plt.show()

The sector-level analysis reveals a substantial performance gap between renewable energy equities and traditional oil & gas companies over the study period from 2012 to 2025.

At the beginning of the sample period, both sectors exhibited relatively similar performance, with a hypothetical investment of $100 remaining close to its initial value through much of the 2015–2017 period. However, beginning around 2018, the renewable energy sector started to significantly outperform the oil & gas sector.

The most dramatic divergence occurred between 2020 and 2023, when renewable energy equities experienced rapid appreciation. During this period, the renewable energy portfolio increased from approximately $300 to more than $1,500 at its peak, substantially exceeding the performance of the oil & gas portfolio. This growth was likely influenced by increased investor interest in clean energy, favorable policy developments, technological advancements, and the broader transition toward sustainable energy solutions.

Despite this strong performance, the renewable energy sector also exhibited considerably higher volatility. Following its peak, the portfolio experienced several significant declines, demonstrating that higher returns were accompanied by greater risk and larger fluctuations in portfolio value.

In contrast, the oil & gas portfolio followed a more stable growth trajectory throughout the study period. While it did not achieve the same level of appreciation as renewable energy stocks, it exhibited smaller fluctuations and more consistent performance, particularly during periods of elevated energy prices and strong demand for fossil fuels.

By the end of the study period, the renewable energy portfolio remained substantially above its starting value and significantly outperformed the oil & gas portfolio in terms of cumulative growth. However, the journey to achieve those returns involved considerably greater volatility and downside risk.

Overall, the results suggest that renewable energy equities offered superior long-term growth potential during the study period, while traditional oil & gas companies provided a more stable but lower-growth investment profile. This finding reinforces one of the central themes of the project: higher return opportunities often come with higher levels of uncertainty and risk.

9.3 Risk-Adjusted Performance¶

Returns alone do not fully measure investment success. Professional investors evaluate how much return was generated relative to the amount of risk required to achieve it. To assess risk-adjusted performance, a simplified Sharpe-style measure is calculated by dividing average return by return volatility.

In [91]:
# ============================================================
# Risk-Adjusted Performance
# ============================================================

risk_adjusted = pd.DataFrame({
    "Average Return":
        stock_returns.mean(),

    "Volatility":
        stock_returns.std()
})

risk_adjusted["Return_to_Risk"] = (
    risk_adjusted["Average Return"]
    /
    risk_adjusted["Volatility"]
)

risk_adjusted = risk_adjusted.sort_values(
    "Return_to_Risk",
    ascending=False
)

risk_adjusted
Out[91]:
Average Return Volatility Return_to_Risk
Ticker
NextEra_Energy 0.0138 0.0569 0.2425
First_Solar 0.0276 0.1567 0.1765
Enphase_Energy 0.0349 0.2415 0.1445
Chevron 0.0082 0.0730 0.1130
ConocoPhillips 0.0106 0.0946 0.1118
ExxonMobil 0.0079 0.0709 0.1111
In [92]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))

colors = [
    "#1f77b4",  # NextEra Energy - Blue
    "#ff7f0e",  # First Solar - Orange
    "#2ca02c",  # Enphase Energy - Green
    "#d62728",  # Chevron - Red
    "#9467bd",  # ExxonMobil - Purple
    "#8c564b"   # ConocoPhillips - Brown
]

ax = sns.barplot(
    x=risk_adjusted["Return_to_Risk"],
    y=risk_adjusted.index,
    palette=colors
)

# Add values at end of bars
for i, value in enumerate(risk_adjusted["Return_to_Risk"]):
    ax.text(
        value + 0.003,
        i,
        f"{value:.3f}",
        va="center",
        fontsize=10,
        fontweight="bold"
    )

plt.title(
    "Risk-Adjusted Performance Ranking (2012–2025)",
    fontsize=14,
    fontweight="bold"
)

plt.xlabel(
